confidence.disclosure: Hasan ibn Ali, the elder grandson of the Prophet Muhammad, is well documented in early sira and tarikh works. The major events of his life - his birth in Medina, his role at the Battle of Siffin, the assassination of his father, his brief caliphate in Kufa, and his peace treaty with Mu'awiya - are firmly placed by year. Specific months and days are sometimes preserved (especially for the assassination of Ali and the treaty with Mu'awiya). His birth year is given variously as 624 or 625 CE.

sources: Ibn Sa'd, Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir, vol. 6

journey.event: Born in Medina to Ali ibn Abi Talib and Fatimah al-Zahra, in the year after the Battle of Uhud (3 AH). His grandfather the Prophet Muhammad himself names him Hasan, says the call to prayer in his ear, and shaves his head on the seventh day, giving silver equal to the weight of his hair as charity.
His grandfather the Prophet Muhammad dies in June 632. A few months later his mother Fatimah also dies. He grows up in the household of his father Ali, alongside his younger brother Husayn.

When the third caliph Uthman is besieged by rebels in his house in Medina, Ali sends Hasan and Husayn to help guard him. Hasan stands at the gate of Uthman's house but is unable to prevent the attackers from entering and killing the caliph.
After his father Ali becomes caliph, accompanies him from Medina to Kufa, which Ali makes the new capital of the caliphate. Lives in Kufa for the rest of his father's reign.

Marches with his father to the Battle of Siffin against the forces of Mu'awiya, on the upper Euphrates in northern Syria. The battle ends in a stalemate followed by the agreement to arbitrate, which leads to the Kharijite secession.
journey.source: Al-Tabari; Nasr ibn Muzahim, Waq'at Siffin

journey.event: In January 661 his father Ali is mortally wounded in the mosque of Kufa by the Kharijite Ibn Muljam, and dies a few days later. Hasan is acclaimed as the new caliph by the people of Kufa. He is the fifth and last of the Rashidun in the Sunni tradition, and the second Imam of Twelver Shi'ism.

Mu'awiya marches an army from Syria towards Iraq. Hasan tries to raise an army from the Kufans but finds them lukewarm. After his vanguard under Ubayd Allah ibn Abbas defects to Mu'awiya, and after Hasan himself is wounded in an attack at Mada'in, he opens negotiations.

Concludes the famous peace treaty with Mu'awiya, abdicating the caliphate on the conditions that Mu'awiya rule by the Quran and the Sunna, that no successor be named after him, that Hasan's followers be safe, and that the rightful inheritance of the family of the Prophet be respected. The year 41 AH is later remembered as the Year of the Community ('Am al-Jama'a) for ending the first civil war.

After the abdication, withdraws from political life and returns to Medina, where he settles in the house of his grandfather. He devotes himself to worship, charity, and teaching, and gains a reputation for great generosity (he is said to have given away his entire fortune more than once for the sake of God).

Lives quietly in Medina for about a decade after his abdication. Marries and divorces a number of women (the high number is reported in later Sunni and Shia sources alike, though the exact figure is debated). Has many children.

Dies in Medina in 669 (49 AH), at about 45 years of age. Tradition (especially Shia tradition) holds that he was poisoned by one of his wives at the instigation of Mu'awiya, who wanted to clear the way for the succession of his son Yazid. Sunni sources are divided on the cause of death.

Is buried in the Baqi cemetery in Medina. He had wished to be buried near his grandfather the Prophet, but to avoid conflict with the Umayyad authorities his brother Husayn buried him next to his mother Fatimah and his grandmother Fatimah bint Asad in al-Baqi.
